cot_prefix: Okay, let's tackle this question. The passage is about how novelists, playwrights, and screenwriters differ in terms of control over their work. The question asks why a novelist can expect the publisher to print the manuscript as written. The options are A, B, C, or D. Looking at the passage, there's a part that says, "The novelist keeps absolute control over his text until it is published and received by the audience." It also mentions that even a well-established novelist can deliver a manuscript expecting it to be printed as is. So this points to the idea that the novelist has that control. Option A says it's because the novelist keeps absolute control. That seems to directly match the text. Option C talks about the novel being limited to a single channel of info (writing). The passage does mention that the novel uses only writing, which might explain why it's easier to print as is compared to plays or films which need actors, sets, etc. But the key point in the passage is the novelist's control until publication. Option D says it's because they're seldom advised by editors. The passage says the novelist may be advised to revise, but if they refuse, no one is surprised. So it's possible to have advice, but the novelist can still choose to not make changes. So D isn't the main reason. So the answer is probably A. But wait, the passage also says that plays and screenplays require more collaboration. The reason a novelist can expect to have their work printed as is is because once it's written, the publisher doesn't need others to interpret it as much as they do for plays or films. But the question specifically asks why the novelist can expect this. The passage says they have absolute control until published, which is option A.
